slides.title: Python Has a VIP Queue
slides.body: When Python sees `2 + 3 * 4`, it doesn't just read left to right — it has **priority rules** that decide which operator goes first. Get this wrong and your math will silently lie to you.
slides.tip: These rules are called operator precedence — the same idea as PEMDAS from school math.

slides.title: The Surprising Result
slides.body: Multiplication beats addition in priority, so `3 * 4` runs first, then `2` is added. The answer is **14**, not 20.
slides.tip: This isn't a bug — it matches standard math rules.

slides.title: The Precedence Ladder
slides.body: Python ranks operators from **highest to lowest priority**: `**` → `* / // %` → `+ -` → comparisons (`==`, `<`, …) → `not` → `and` → `or`. Higher rank goes first.
slides.tip: ** (exponentiation) is the highest — and the one exception that goes right-to-left.

slides.title: Parentheses Override Everything
slides.body: Wrap anything in `()` and Python evaluates it **first**, no matter what. Parentheses are your clearest, safest tool for controlling order.
slides.tip: Even when not strictly needed, parentheses make your intent obvious to anyone reading your code.

slides.title: You've Got the Order Down!
slides.body: Python always follows the same ladder: **exponentiation → multiply/divide → add/subtract → comparisons → logical**. When in doubt, add `()` — clarity beats cleverness every time.
slides.tip: A classic trap: `score1 + score2 + score3 / 3` divides only score3. Fix it: `(score1 + score2 + score3) / 3`.
